# US power use to beat record highs in 2026 and 2027 as AI use surges, EIA says

By [Scott Disavino](https://www.reuters.com/authors/scott-disavino/)

July 7, 2026 5:00 PM UTC Updated 3 hours ago

*    
*    
*    

*    
*    
*    
*    
*    

![Image 2: Centerpoint Energy Substation](https://www.reuters.com/resizer/v2/2ZUHF3XFEJOF5EAS5LLECUS6KM.jpg?auth=54f1b1b524025fd054839f8c0645c5e8f75dcfef5fb00456d1c8d663a3fb2a61&width=1920&quality=80)

Electric transmission towers at a Centerpoint Energy substation in Bellaire near Houston, Texas, U.S., May 13, 2026. REUTERS/Shahrzad Rasekh/File Photo [Purchase Licensing Rights, opens new tab](https://www.reutersconnect.com/item/centerpoint-energy-substation/dGFnOnJldXRlcnMuY29tLDIwMjY6bmV3c21sX1JDMkc4TEFNN01WVA%3D%3D/?utm_medium=rcom-article-media&utm_campaign=rcom-rcp-lead)

*   Summary

*   Commercial electricity demand is expected to outpace residential demand in 2026 for the first time on record
*   Renewables' generation share rises from around 24% in 2025 to 27% in 2027, the outlook ​said
*   Residential power sales seen dipping in 2026 after hitting a 2025 record

NEW YORK, July 7 (Reuters) - U.S. power consumption, which hit its second straight annual record high in 2025, will rise further in 2026 and 2027, driven by AI-hungry data centers and electrification, the Energy Information Administration (EIA) said in its Short-Term Energy ​Outlook (STEO) on Tuesday.

The EIA projected power demand will rise from a record 4,195 billion ​kilowatt-hours (kWh) in 2025 to 4,269 billion kWh in 2026 and 4,399 billion kWh ⁠in 2027.

The Reuters Power Up newsletter provides everything you need to know about the global energy industry. Sign up [here.](https://www.reuters.com/newsletters/reuters-power-up/?location=article-paragraph&redirectUrl=%2Fbusiness%2Fenergy%2Fus-power-use-beat-record-highs-2026-2027-ai-use-surges-eia-says-2026-07-07%2F)

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

Report Ad![Image 3](blob:http://localhost/a2f9abce2c7bd7e72f7c1065e8e25533)

Demand is surging in large part due to data centers dedicated to artificial intelligence and ​cryptocurrency, and as homes and businesses use more electricity and less fossil fuels for heat and transportation.

Electricity ​demand growth is led by an increase in the commercial sector, which is expected to outpace residential demand in 2026 for the first time on record, the agency said.

The EIA forecast power sales in 2026 will ease to ​1,508 billion kWh for residential consumers, but rise to 1,550 billion kWh for commercial customers and ​1,065 billion kWh for industrial customers.

Those forecasts compare with all-time highs of 1,515 billion kWh for residential consumers ‌and 1,493 ⁠billion kWh for commercial customers in 2025, and 1,064 billion kWh for industrial customers in 2000.

Advertisement · Scroll to continue

As renewable output rises, the EIA said the share of power generation from coal will slide from 17% in 2025 to 15% in 2026 and 2027, while the share of natural gas will hold ​at 40% in 2026 and ​2027, the same ⁠as in 2025.

The percentage of renewable generation will rise from around 24% in 2025 to 25% in 2026 and 27% in 2027, while nuclear power's ​share will hold at 18% in 2026 and 2027, the same as ​in 2025, according ⁠to the outlook.

The EIA projected gas sales in 2026 would slide to 12.5 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) for residential consumers and 9.5 bcfd for commercial customers, but rise to 24.0 bcfd for industrial ⁠customers and ​36.6 bcfd for power generation.

Those figures compare with all-time ​highs of 14.3 bcfd in 1996 for residential consumers, 9.9 bcfd in 2025 for commercial customers, 23.8 bcfd in 1973 for ​industrial customers, and 36.8 bcfd in 2024 for power generation.
